scenes.title: Conversation memory
scenes.tagline: Rig PR #1702 — chat history becomes a framework primitive
scenes.footnote: +1,903 / −4   ·   16 files   ·   rig-core + a new rig-memory crate

beats.narration: A close look at a real pull request -- against Rig, the Rust framework for L-L-M applications. Pull request seventeen-oh-two.
Its subject is conversation memory. Until this change, every Rig application had to hand-manage its own chat history. This pull request makes memory a first-class, framework-managed concern.
Sixteen files; about nineteen hundred lines added. A new module inside rig-core, and a brand-new companion crate.

nodes.label: ConversationMemory
nodes.sub: the new trait — load / append / clear

beats.narration: The change has three parts. First, a contract -- the ConversationMemory trait, in a new file: rig-core, source, memory dot r-s.
Second, a default implementation -- an in-memory, HashMap-backed backend -- so the feature works out of the box, with nothing to configure.
Third, the wiring. The agent builder and the prompt loop are both threaded with memory: on every prompt, history is loaded before, and the finished turn appended after.
And the opinionated parts -- the named history-shaping policies -- are pushed into a separate companion crate, rig-memory, so rig-core itself stays lean.

scenes.heading: The whole trait, in three methods

beats.narration: Here is the entire contract. Three methods -- load, append, clear -- each one keyed by a conversation I-D string.
load fetches the prior history for a conversation. Rig calls it automatically, before the prompt is ever sent to the model.
append writes a finished turn back. Notice the trait says nothing about where -- a HashMap, Postgres, Redis. That is precisely the point: storage is now pluggable.
And every method returns a WASM-compatible boxed future, rather than a raw async function -- so the trait stays object-safe, and Rig still compiles to WebAssembly.

scenes.heading: How the prompt loop changed

beats.narration: The wiring lives in the prompt loop. Before, the request simply used whatever history the caller passed -- a single line.
After, it resolves history through a match. An explicit with-history call still wins, untouched -- so existing caller-managed code does not change behavior at all.

But when the caller passes no history, and both a memory backend and a conversation I-D are present, the loop loads the prior history itself. A load failure is fatal -- the request cannot proceed without the history it was promised.

After the turn succeeds, the new messages are appended back. This one is best-effort: a failed append only logs a warning, and the model's answer is still returned. Two failure modes -- and the asymmetry is deliberate.
What does this ripple into? Reassuringly little. The API change is purely additive -- not one existing function signature was altered.
Memory failures are funneled through Rig's existing error variants -- so downstream code that exhaustively matches on those errors keeps compiling, with no new arms to handle.
The failure semantics, fatal on load and best-effort on append, are applied identically across both the streaming and non-streaming prompt paths -- no drift between them.

And the crate split is the real discipline. The trait and default backend live in core; the opinionated policies live in rig-memory. The same trait surface is built to host Postgres or Redis backends later, with no further core changes.

scenes.heading: PR #1702, in four ideas
points: Conversation history becomes a trait — load, append, clear.
A default in-memory backend; the prompt loop wires it in for you.
Load failures are fatal; append failures only warn — by design.
Opinionated policies live in a companion crate; rig-core stays lean.

beats.narration: So, pull request seventeen-oh-two, in four ideas. Conversation history becomes a single trait -- load, append, clear.
A default in-memory backend ships with it, and the prompt loop wires it in automatically.
Load failures are fatal; append failures only warn -- a deliberate, considered asymmetry.
And the opinionated policies are kept in a companion crate, so rig-core stays lean. A clean, additive, well-scoped change -- the kind a reviewer is glad to approve.
